description.task: Implement entity mapping annotations from scratch using a custom annotation processor. Create @Entity, @Id, @Column annotations and a mapper that generates SQL DDL from annotated classes.
instructions: Add constructor and getters to Book.
generateCreateTableSQL() reads @Entity.tableName(), iterates declared fields, and builds valid SQL. @Id field gets 'INT PRIMARY KEY', @Column fields use the name, length, and nullable attributes.
generateInsertSQL() reads field values via reflection and generates an INSERT statement.
Output should be valid-looking SQL strings.
description.hint: Hibernate (the JPA implementation) does exactly this — it reads @Entity/@Column annotations and generates DDL automatically. This is ORM in a nutshell.
description.expectedOutput:
CREATE TABLE books (id INT PRIMARY KEY, book_title VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL, author_name VARCHAR(255), price VARCHAR(255));
INSERT INTO books (id, book_title, author_name, price) VALUES (1, 'Effective Java', 'Joshua Bloch', '49.99');

description.task: Implement a one-to-many relationship between two entity classes (Department has many Employees) and demonstrate how to navigate the relationship in both directions.
instructions: Add setDepartment(), getDepartment(), getId(), getName() to Employee.
Run Main as-is — no other changes needed.
Verify both sides of the relationship work (department -> employees, employee -> department).
description.hint: In JPA, @OneToMany and @ManyToOne annotations map these relationships. The mappedBy attribute specifies the owning side of the relationship.
description.expectedOutput:
Engineering employees:
  Alice
  Bob
Alice works in: Engineering
Engineering employee count: 2

description.task: Demonstrate Lazy vs Eager loading strategies in Java. Implement two types of proxy-based associations and show the timing difference in when data is loaded.
description.context: JPA's FetchType.LAZY defers loading associated data until first access. FetchType.EAGER loads everything upfront. Choosing correctly affects performance significantly.
instructions: The code is mostly complete. Run Main as-is.
Understand and verify: with EAGER, loading happens at creation time. With LAZY, loading message should NOT appear until getBooks() is accessed.
The LazyList already simulates this — since setBooks() calls initData(), the lazy load message won't appear (data is pre-set). Trace the flow.
Add one additional lazy author WITHOUT calling setBooks() and verify the lazy load message appears when accessing size().
description.hint: In JPA: @OneToMany(fetch = FetchType.LAZY) — default for collections. @ManyToOne(fetch = FetchType.EAGER) — default for single entities. Always prefer LAZY for collections.
description.expectedOutput:
--- EAGER Loading ---
[EAGER] Loading author 'Joshua Bloch' AND all books immediately
Author created. Accessing books: [Effective Java, Java Puzzlers]

--- LAZY Loading ---
[LAZY] Loading author 'Martin Fowler' only
Author created. Not accessed books yet.
Now accessing: 2 books
